← → arrow keys · ESC menu
Music Research Methods · Northwestern Bienen School of Music

Bayesian Thinking
for Music Researchers

From prior beliefs to posterior knowledge — an interactive introduction to probabilistic reasoning through musical examples.

P(H | D) = P(D | H) · P(H) / P(D)

USE ARROW KEYS OR → TO ADVANCE

Module 01 · Introduction

Why Probability?

What does it mean to "know" something in music research?

Music researchers make probabilistic claims constantly: "Composers in the common practice preferred stepwise motion." "Listeners expect tonic resolution." "Jazz soloists target chord tones on strong beats." These are statements about frequencies, tendencies, and expectations — which means they are inherently statistical.

"The frequentist asks: what is the probability of this data given the hypothesis? The Bayesian asks: what is the probability of the hypothesis given this data?"
Frequentist

Probability = long-run frequency. Parameters are fixed; data is random. Asks P(data | H₀) and produces p-values, confidence intervals, and reject/fail-to-reject decisions.

Bayesian

Probability = degree of belief. Parameters have distributions. Asks P(H | data) and produces posteriors and credible intervals, updating on evidence.

Core Vocabulary · ① Prior
P(H) · What you believe before seeing the data
The Prior
The prior encodes everything you know — or assume — before new evidence arrives. It may come from prior studies, domain expertise, or be deliberately vague (uninformative).
Musical Example
Before analyzing Schubert lieder, you believe roughly 50/50 that any phrase ends on a half vs. full cadence. That uncertainty is your prior: P(full cadence) = 0.5
Studio Example
A teacher believes 40% of incoming students are consistent self-practicers, based on years of experience — an informative prior encoding real knowledge.
Uninformative vs. Informative
A flat prior, e.g. Beta(1,1), says "I have no idea." An informative prior, e.g. Beta(8,2), encodes a strong expectation of high values. Neither is more "objective" — choosing a flat prior is still a choice.
Core Vocabulary · ② Likelihood
P(D | H) · How well the hypothesis predicts the data
The Likelihood
The likelihood asks: if the hypothesis were true, how probable would this specific data be? High likelihood means the data "fits" the hypothesis well. Notice the direction — P(data | hypothesis), not the reverse.
Musical Example
Hypothesis: "This piece is in A major." Data: F# appears frequently.
P(F# frequent | A major) is high — F# is the 6th scale degree. The data fits the hypothesis well.
Studio Example
Hypothesis: "Student understands scales." Data: student passes a written test.
P(pass | understands) = 0.90. Understanding usually leads to passing — high likelihood.
The Key Direction
Likelihood is P(data | hypothesis) — not P(hypothesis | data). That inversion is exactly what Bayes' theorem achieves.
Core Vocabulary · ③ Posterior
P(H | D) · What you believe after seeing the data
The Posterior
The posterior is the goal — your updated belief about the hypothesis having incorporated new evidence. It balances the prior with how well the hypothesis predicted the data. Today's posterior is tomorrow's prior.
Musical Example
You started 50/50 on cadence types. After analyzing 40 Schubert phrases and finding 28 full cadences:
P(full cadence | data) ≈ 0.71. Sharper, more informed belief.
Studio Example
The teacher sees daily practice for 3 weeks. Posterior rises 0.40 → 0.85 that this is a consistent practicer. Evidence accumulates.
The Bayesian Loop
Each study produces a posterior. That posterior becomes the prior for the next study. Science accumulates formally — not through a series of independent binary verdicts.
Core Vocabulary · ④ Marginal Likelihood + The Formula
P(D) · How surprising is this data overall?
The Marginal Likelihood
A normalizing constant — the average probability of the data across all hypotheses. In practice: P(D|H)·P(H) + P(D|¬H)·P(¬H). When comparing two hypotheses it cancels out and can be ignored.

How the four terms connect

Prior
P(H)
×
Likelihood
P(D|H)
÷
Evidence
P(D)
=
Posterior
P(H|D)
P(H | D) = [ P(D | H) × P(H) ] / P(D)

Start with what you believed → multiply by how well the hypothesis predicted the data → divide by how likely the data was in general → updated belief.

Core Vocabulary · Putting It Together

A Studio Scenario

All four terms in action

① Prior · P(H)

A teacher thinks 30% of new students persist past year 1 — her base rate from experience.

② Likelihood · P(D|H)

Student practices every day this week. Students who persist do this 80% of the time; those who quit, only 20%.

③ Evidence · P(D)

P(daily practice) = 0.80×0.30 + 0.20×0.70 = 0.38

④ Posterior · P(H|D)

P(persist | daily practice) = (0.80 × 0.30) / 0.38 = 0.63
30% prior → 63% posterior after one good week

Key Insight
The posterior (63%) is much higher than the prior (30%), but less than the likelihood ratio alone would suggest. The 30% base rate acts as a brake — one good week isn't conclusive. This is the base rate in action.
Module 02 · Freq vs. Bayes

Two Philosophies

The same musical question — two fundamentally different answers

Frequentist
  • Data is random; parameters are fixed
  • Probability = long-run frequency
  • Tests P(data | H₀)
  • Produces: p-values, CIs, reject/fail-to-reject
  • Requires imagining repeated experiments
  • Needs power analysis & effect sizes
Bayesian
  • Parameters have distributions
  • Probability = degree of belief
  • Computes P(H | data)
  • Produces: posteriors, credible intervals, BFs
  • Works on the data you actually have
  • Requires prior specification
Why It Matters for Music Research
When we say "Bach preferred authentic cadences," we're making a claim about P(hypothesis) — a Bayesian statement. Frequentist p-values can't directly support that. They only tell you whether the data would be unusual if the null were true.
Module 02 · The Update Rule

How the Beta Update Works

α and β are just counts — the update rule is addition

What α and β mean

Think of α as a running tally of authentic cadences — including any pseudo-observations from your prior. β tallies everything else.

The prior Beta(2,2) says: "I've effectively seen 2 authentic and 2 non-authentic before we start." That's all.

The update rule
Observe authentic → α += 1
Observe other     → β += 1
That's the entire rule.
Step-by-step: starting from Beta(2,2), observing A · A · O · A
START
Prior
α=2 · β=2
Beta(2,2)
mean = 0.50
obs 1: A
α += 1
α=3 · β=2
Beta(3,2)
mean = 0.60
obs 2: A
α += 1
α=4 · β=2
Beta(4,2)
mean = 0.67
obs 3: O
β += 1
α=4 · β=3
Beta(4,3)
mean = 0.57
obs 4: A
α += 1
α=5 · β=3
Beta(5,3)
mean = 0.63
General rule (n observations, k authentic) Prior Beta(α, β)
α_post = α + k
β_post = β + (n − k)
Mean = α_post / (α_post + β_post)
Applied to the Bach example → Prior Beta(2, 2)
α_post = 2 + 32 = 34
β_post = 2 + 8  = 10
Mean = 34 / 44 ≈ 0.77
Module 02 · Example 1

Do Baroque Composers Prefer Authentic Cadences?

40 cadences from a Bach chorale: 32 authentic, 8 other

Frequentist

Binomial test against H₀: p = 0.5

H₀: p = 0.5, n=40, k=32
p-value ≈ 0.0008 → Reject H₀

But what does rejecting H₀ tell us about Bach's actual preference rate?

Bayesian

Model p as unknown. Prior Beta(2,2), update.

Prior: Beta(2,2)
Data: k=32, n=40 → Posterior: Beta(2+32, 2+8) = Beta(34,10)
E[p | data] = 34/(34+10) ≈ 0.773

95% Credible Interval
Find θlo, θhi such that P(θlo < p < θhi | data) = 0.95
i.e. 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles of Beta(34,10)
→ solved numerically via inverse incomplete Beta function
[0.63, 0.89]
Unlike a frequentist CI, this interval makes a direct probability statement: there is a 95% posterior probability that Bach's true rate lies in [0.63, 0.89]. No repeated-sampling interpretation required.

"Bach uses authentic cadences ~77% of the time."

The Key Difference
p-value = P(data | H₀). Posterior = P(H | data). The latter is what music researchers actually want to know.
Module 02 · Example 2

Are Minor-Key Pieces Rated as Sadder?

60 participants: major 8/30 sad, minor 22/30 sad

Frequentist
χ²(1) = 12.27, p = 0.0005
Cramér's V = 0.45
Significant association ✓

But the p-value doesn't tell us how probable it is that minor is sadder.

Bayesian
p_major | data ~ Beta(9,23)
p_minor | data ~ Beta(23,9)
P(p_minor > p_major | data) ≈ 0.9997

"99.97% posterior probability minor is rated sadder."

Direct Probability Statement
This is what listeners, performers, and teachers actually want to know — and it's only expressible in Bayesian terms. A p-value can't say "there's a 99.97% chance minor is sadder."

→ But wait: we used flat priors Beta(1,1) for both conditions — as if we had no prior knowledge. Is that realistic? Next slide explores what happens when we use what the literature actually tells us.

Module 02 · Example 2 continued

What If We Use Informed Priors?

Encoding Hevner (1935) and Kastner & Crowder (1990) before seeing the data

Decades of music psychology tell us minor keys are consistently rated sadder and major keys happier. Using the same flat Beta(1,1) for both conditions pretends we don't know this. Let's encode what we actually know:

Flat Priors (previous slide)
Prior major: Beta(1,1) → mean = 0.50
Prior minor: Beta(1,1) → mean = 0.50

Posterior major: Beta(9,23) → mean = 0.28
Posterior minor: Beta(23,9) → mean = 0.72
P(minor sadder | data) ≈ 0.9997

"I have no idea going in." Honest if you're new to the field — unrealistic if you've read Hevner.

Informed Priors (literature-based)
Prior major: Beta(2,6) → mean = 0.25
Prior minor: Beta(6,4) → mean = 0.60

Posterior major: Beta(10,28) → mean = 0.26
Posterior minor: Beta(28,12) → mean = 0.70
P(minor sadder | data) ≈ 0.9999

"Prior research says minor skews sad, major skews happy." The prior encodes real knowledge.

The Key Lesson: Data Overwhelms Weak Priors
With n=30 per condition, both prior choices lead to nearly identical posteriors and the same substantive conclusion. The informed prior shifts the means only slightly (0.28→0.26 for major, 0.72→0.70 for minor) and nudges the probability from 0.9997 to 0.9999. When you have enough data, reasonable prior choices don't matter much. This is one of the most reassuring features of Bayesian analysis — and a direct answer to the "your prior is arbitrary" reviewer objection.
When priors DO matter
Priors have real influence when n is small (e.g. n=5 per condition), when effects are subtle, or when the prior is very strong relative to the data. This is why sensitivity analysis — showing results across a range of priors — is best practice in Bayesian reporting.
Module 02 · Example 3

Do Musicians Recall Pitch Better?

Musicians: 14/20 correct · Non-musicians: 9/20 correct

Frequentist (small n problem)
Two-proportion z-test
z = 1.89, p = 0.059
→ Fail to reject H₀ at α=.05
95% CI (difference): [−0.01, 0.51]
CI crosses zero → "inconclusive" by frequentist standard
Bayesian (prior from literature)
Prior musicians: Beta(3,2) → mean = 0.60
Prior non-musicians: Beta(1,1) → mean = 0.50
Posterior musicians: Beta(17,8) → mean = 0.68
Posterior non-musicians: Beta(11,13) → mean = 0.46
95% CrI musicians: [0.46, 0.85]
95% CrI non-musicians: [0.26, 0.67]
P(musicians better | data) ≈ 0.91
Prior source
The prior Beta(3,2) for musicians encodes a weak expectation of above-chance performance (~60%), consistent with Sloboda (1985) on musicians' superior tonal encoding and Krumhansl & Shepard (1979) on trained listeners' key-profile sensitivity. It contributes the equivalent of 5 pseudo-observations — modest enough to be overridden by a moderate dataset.
Posterior Distributions — note the wide, overlapping credible intervals
■ Musicians Beta(17,8) — 95% CrI [0.46, 0.85] ■ Non-musicians Beta(11,13) — 95% CrI [0.26, 0.67] shaded = 95% credible interval
The Small-N Problem
p=0.059 → frequentist says "not significant." But the Bayesian posteriors tell a richer story: the distributions overlap substantially (hence only 91% probability, not 99%), and both CrIs are wide — honestly reflecting that n=20 per group can't tightly constrain a proportion. This is the right conclusion from small data, not a false negative.
Module 03 · Bayes' Theorem

Bayes' Theorem

Breaking down the formula through music

P(H | D) = [ P(D | H) × P(H) ] / P(D)

P(H | D) — Posterior
Updated belief after data.
e.g. "How likely is this piece in A major, given these note frequencies?"

P(D | H) — Likelihood
Probability of data if H were true.
e.g. "If A major, how likely is F# to be frequent?"

P(H) — Prior
Belief before seeing data.
e.g. "Base rate of major keys in common practice: ~60%"

P(D) — Marginal Likelihood
How likely is this data across all hypotheses?
P(D|H)·P(H) + P(D|¬H)·P(¬H)

Module 03 · Interactive Widget

Musical Key Detection via Bayes

Click notes present in an excerpt. Posteriors update across all 24 keys using Krumhansl-Kessler profiles as likelihoods.

Widget 01 · Key Detection

Pitch Class Selector

Click to toggle notes present in the excerpt:

Top Key Candidates (Posterior Probability)
Select notes above to begin analysis
Module 04 · Exercise 1

Melody Recognition

Is "I recognize this" reliable evidence of familiarity?

A participant says "I recognize this melody." Known rates: true recognition 85%, false alarm 15%. Base rate of truly familiar melodies in the set: 30%.

P(FM) = 0.30, P(NF) = 0.70
P("recognize" | FM) = 0.85
P("recognize" | NF) = 0.15

P("recognize") = 0.85×0.30 + 0.15×0.70 = 0.360

P(FM | "recognize") = 0.255 / 0.360 ≈ 0.708
Implication
Even when a listener says they recognize a melody, there's a 29% chance it's false recognition — because truly familiar items are rare. Self-reported recognition alone is insufficient evidence. The base rate dominates in low-prevalence situations.
Module 04 · Exercise 2

Huron's Post-Skip Reversal

Melodic expectation meets stylistic context

A corpus of 500 folk melodies: after a large ascending leap (>P5), the next note descends 78% of the time. But this piece looks like a military march (base rate: 40% post-leap ascent or continuation).

P(descend | corpus) = 0.78
P(descend | march style) = 0.60
Prior P(march style) = 0.25

Mixed prior = 0.25×0.60 + 0.75×0.78
= 0.15 + 0.585 = 0.735
Bayesian Melodic Expectation
73.5% confidence in descending motion — lower than the raw corpus rate (78%) because stylistic context adjusts the prior. Bayesian reasoning combines corpus statistics with contextual knowledge formally, mirroring how expert listeners actually process expectations.
Module 04 · Exercise 3

Composer Attribution

Haydn or Mozart? Sequential updating over two features

A newly discovered piece has fp dynamic markings. Haydn uses fp: 40%; Mozart: 65%. Prior: 50/50.

P(Haydn | fp) = (0.40×0.50) / (0.40×0.50 + 0.65×0.50) = 0.381
P(Mozart | fp) = 0.619

Now add Sturm und Drang chromaticism (Haydn: 70%, Mozart: 30%). Update again using the posterior above as the new prior:

P(SuD) = 0.70×0.381 + 0.30×0.619 = 0.453
P(Haydn | fp, SuD) = (0.70×0.381) / 0.453 = 0.588
Sequential Updating
fp markings tip toward Mozart; SuD chromaticism tips back toward Haydn. Each feature is a Bayesian update — today's posterior is tomorrow's prior. This is formally equivalent to adding both features simultaneously.
Module 04 · Exercise 4

Jazz Improvisation

Predicting the next chord under harmonic ambiguity

An improviser hears an ambiguous chord — 60% likely a ii, 40% likely a ♭II (tritone sub). From a corpus of ii-V-I standards (1940–1960):

  • After ii: V follows 75%, I follows 10%, other 15%
  • After ♭II: V follows 55%, I follows 25%, other 20%
P(V | ii) = 0.75, P(V | ♭II) = 0.55
P(ii) = 0.60, P(♭II) = 0.40

P(V next) = 0.75×0.60 + 0.55×0.40
= 0.450 + 0.220 = 0.670
Mixture Models in Improvisation
The improviser should plan for V with 67% confidence — a Bayesian mixture model under harmonic uncertainty. This underlies computational approaches to jazz chord prediction and automatic chord recognition.
Module 05 · Interactive Widget

Sequential Belief Updating

Click cadence types one at a time. The posterior updates with each observation. Adjust the prior slider first to encode what you know before starting.

Widget 02 · Cadence Updater
Prior: P(authentic) =
0.50
Posterior P(authentic | observations)
0.50
95% CrI: —
Observations: 0
posterior mean 95% credible interval — narrows as evidence accumulates
Module 05 · The Model

The Beta-Binomial Model

The math behind sequential updating

When observations are binary (authentic / not authentic), the conjugate prior is the Beta distribution, making updating trivially simple:

Prior: Beta(α, β)
Data: k successes out of n trials
Posterior: Beta(α + k, β + n − k)

E[p | data] = (α + k) / (α + β + n)

Flat prior: Beta(1,1) — "I have no idea." Posterior mean = k/n, identical to the raw proportion.

Informative prior: Beta(8,2) — "I expect ~80% authentic." Pulls the posterior toward 0.80 until enough data accumulates to override it.

Conjugacy
When prior and posterior belong to the same distribution family, we call this a conjugate prior. The Beta-Binomial is the canonical example. Other useful pairs: Dirichlet-Multinomial (interval distributions), Normal-Normal (continuous measures like reaction time).
Module 06 · Corpus Studies Widget

Intervallic Preference Across Corpora

Prior from Huron (2006) folk melody corpus. Adjust sliders to represent new corpus findings, then update the posterior.

Widget 03 · Dirichlet-Multinomial Update

Prior (Huron 2006 folk melodies):

New corpus — adjust sliders:

Corpus n
50 obs
← drag left to see uncertainty grow, right to see it shrink

Posterior (combined):

▬ point estimate  |  shaded region = 95% CrI  |  ticks = CrI bounds
Wide shading = high uncertainty (few observations). Narrow shading = well-constrained. The shaded region shows the plausible range for the true rate — not just the point estimate.
Module 06 · Corpus Studies

Bayesian vs. Frequentist Corpus Claims

"Stepwise motion is preferred in tonal melodies"

Frequentist Corpus Study
  • Count intervals, report proportions
  • Test against null (uniform distribution)
  • Each corpus treated independently
  • Large corpora always "significant" — even 0.3% effects
  • Can't say "90% confident stepwise > 40%"
Bayesian Corpus Study
  • Dirichlet-Multinomial model
  • Prior = prior literature formally encoded
  • Evidence accumulates across corpora
  • Small corpora contribute without overwhelming
  • "P(stepwise > 40%) = 0.95" — direct claim
Your Literature Review Is Your Prior
Bayesian statistics gives you a formal framework to make this explicit: "Based on studies A, B, C, I set prior Dirichlet(α). My corpus updates this to…" The next researcher uses your posterior as their prior. Science accumulates.
Module 08 · Music Education · Exercise 8.1

Is This Student Ready to Advance?

A studio teacher's diagnostic problem

True-ready students pass a readiness checklist 90% of the time. Not-yet-ready students pass 40% of the time. Base rate of truly ready at this stage: 35%. Student passes the checklist.

Likelihood values adapted from Fiske (1977) — Sequential Assessment in Music Learning; base rate from McPherson & Zimmerman (2011) — Self-regulation of musical learning.

What is P(truly ready | passed checklist)? Guess before calculating:

P(pass) = 0.90×0.35 + 0.40×0.65 = 0.575
P(ready | pass) = (0.90×0.35) / 0.575 ≈ 0.548

→ See next slide to watch a second assessment update this posterior.

Module 08 · Music Education · Exercise 8.1 continued

A Second Assessment

Using the checklist posterior as a new prior

The checklist gave us P(ready) ≈ 0.548. Now the teacher adds a sight-reading test. True-ready students pass sight-reading 70% of the time; not-yet-ready students pass 20% of the time. Student passes.

Step 1 — new prior
P(ready) = 0.548
P(not ready) = 0.452
(yesterday's posterior)
Step 2 — likelihoods
P(pass SR | ready) = 0.70
P(pass SR | not ready) = 0.20
Step 3 — marginal P(pass SR)
= 0.70 × 0.548
+ 0.20 × 0.452
= 0.3836 + 0.0904
= 0.474
Step 4 — posterior
P(ready | pass SR)
= (0.70 × 0.548) / 0.474
= 0.3836 / 0.474
≈ 0.809
From 54.8% to 80.9% with one more test
Each independent assessment multiplies the evidence. The key intuition: the second test is more informative than the first because the first already screened out many clearly-not-ready students — the population the second test sees is already enriched. This is why sequential assessment beats a single high-stakes evaluation.
Module 08 · Exercise 8.2 · Interactive Widget

Practice Consistency Tracker

Log weekly practice observations. Consistent practicer (CP): 5–7 days/wk. Inconsistent (IP): 1–3 days/wk.

Widget 05 · Studio Practice Model
Prior: P(consistent) =
0.50
P(consistent practicer | logs)
Consistent: 0.50
Inconsistent: 0.50
Weeks logged: 0
⚠ point estimate only. This model returns a single number, not a distribution. The true uncertainty is larger than the needle suggests — it depends directly on how well-calibrated the assumed likelihood values (85% / 25%) actually are for this student. Those values are population averages, not measured facts. A model limitation, not a data limitation.
Module 08 · Exercise 8.3

Concert Attendance in K–12

12 of 40 students came to an optional concert — cause for alarm?

A colleague says: "Only 30% attended — students aren't engaged." But district records show optional concerts historically draw 25–35%.

Step 1 — encode the prior
District records: concerts draw 25–35%, centre ~30%.
Encode as Beta(6, 14) — like having seen
6 attend and 14 not at past concerts.
E[p] = 6/(6+14) = 0.30 ✓
ESS = 20 pseudo-observations
Step 2 — add the data
Observed: 12 attended, 28 did not.
Beta-Binomial update rule:
Beta(6+12, 14+28) = Beta(18, 42)
α counts attendances; β counts absences
Step 3 — posterior mean
E[p] = α/(α+β) = 18/60 = 0.300
Unchanged — data confirmed the prior exactly
Step 4 — credible interval
For Beta(18, 42), the 2.5th and 97.5th
percentiles (via numerical inverse CDF):
95% CrI: [0.19, 0.43]
No closed form — solved by bisection on the regularised incomplete Beta function
Key Insight
The data are entirely consistent with the historical baseline. A frequentist test against H₀: p=0.5 gives p≈0.02 — "significant!" — but that null is irrelevant. The Bayesian prior encodes the right baseline and reveals no anomaly exists.

Extension: Of 28 non-attenders, 15 cited a conflict, 13 said uninterested. P(engaged-but-unavailable | "conflict") = (0.90×0.50)/(0.90×0.50+0.30×0.50) = 0.75. Follow up with them differently than the disengaged group.

Module 08 · Exercise 8.4 · Interactive Widget

Wrong Note Diagnosis

A beginner plays B♭ instead of B♮ consistently. Four hypotheses. Adjust evidence sliders — posteriors update live.

Widget 06 · Error Diagnosis
H1: Key sig confusion
H2: Muscle memory
H3: Note reading error
H4: Technique issue
Hesitates at key sig
5
Error in fast passages
5
Error in new music
5
Vanishes when memorized
5
Likelihood matrix — each cell = P(evidence | hypothesis) · updates live
high likelihood low likelihood neutral
Posterior over hypotheses
Module 08 · Exercise 8.5 · Quiz

The "Talented" Student Problem

How reliable is a teacher's first impression?

A teacher correctly identifies high-aptitude students 80% of the time. Labels average students "talented" 25% of the time. In a typical class, 20% are high-aptitude. A student is labeled "talented." What is P(truly high-aptitude)?

P("talented") = 0.80×0.20 + 0.25×0.80 = 0.36
P(high | "talented") = (0.80×0.20) / 0.36 = 0.444

Equity implication: Most "talented" labels are false positives because high aptitude is rare. Adding a sight-singing test raises posterior to 0.80. First impressions systematically disadvantage quiet, less performatively-confident students.

Module 08 · Exercise 8.6 · Interactive Widget

Lesson Persistence Model

Base rate: 60% of students continue past year 1. Adjust the student profile — posterior updates based on research-backed likelihood ratios.

Widget 07 · Dropout Model
P(persists to year 2)
0.60
Module 09 · Reporting · Language

Reporting: Language & Claims

Never interpret a frequentist result in Bayesian language, or vice versa

Valid Frequentist Statements
  • "Significant at α=.05 (p=.023)"
  • "We reject the null of no preference"
  • "95% CI: [.61, .84]"
  • "Cohen's d=0.62, medium effect"
  • "Post-hoc power = 78% at d=0.5"
Valid Bayesian Statements
  • "Posterior probability effect is positive: .94"
  • "95% credible interval: [.63, .88]"
  • "Prior shifted from .50 to .79"
  • "BF₁₀=8.3 — strong evidence for H₁"
  • "P(authentic >60% | corpus) = 0.91"
What "Significant" Actually Means
Statistical significance = "This result would be unlikely if the null were true." It says nothing about effect size, probability the hypothesis is true, or musical relevance. A corpus of 50,000 notes will make every tiny preference "significant" — always pair with effect size.
Module 09 · Reporting · Common Errors

Four Errors to Avoid in Every Manuscript

The most common mistakes in music research writing

⚠ Common Errors

❌ Misreading a p-value:

"There is a 95% probability the true effect is positive."

A CI is not a probability statement about the parameter for this specific study.

❌ Calling a credible interval "significant":

"The result was significant (95% CI [.61, .84])."

Bayesian credible intervals have no significance threshold.

❌ Non-significant = no effect:

"Since p=.12, we conclude there is no preference."

Only a Bayes Factor or equivalence test can support a null claim.

❌ Calling a posterior "significant":

"The Bayesian analysis was significant (posterior=.94)."

Report posterior probability and credible interval. No threshold.

Module 09 · Reporting · Sample Size Widget

What Does N Actually Buy You?

Watch the 95% credible interval shrink as sample size increases. The posterior density draws in real time.

Widget · Posterior Precision vs. N

20
Observed proportion =
0.65

—

Module 09 · Reporting · Checklist

Reporting Checklist

Before submitting to Music Perception, EMR, JRME, PLOS ONE…

Frequentist
Bayesian
Universal (both frameworks)
Module 09 · Reporting · Reviewer Responses

Responding to Reviewers

Common objections to Bayesian papers — and how to answer them

"Your prior is arbitrary — this invalidates the analysis."▶
Run a sensitivity analysis across several reasonable priors. If conclusions hold, the choice doesn't matter. Note that frequentist analysis also embeds arbitrary choices (the null, α, test statistic) — they're just less visible. Cite Gelman et al. (2013).
"Why not just report p-values? Readers won't understand Bayes Factors."▶
Offer supplementary frequentist results. Explain BF briefly: "BF₁₀=8.3 means the data are 8.3× more likely under H₁ than H₀ (Jeffreys 1961: strong evidence)." Provide a BF interpretation table in an appendix.
"Your sample size is too small for this analysis."▶
Small samples produce wide credible intervals — which honestly reflect uncertainty. The Bayesian framework with an informative prior is actually more conservative with small N than NHST. Respond: "The credible interval accurately characterizes our uncertainty. We treat this as a pilot estimate."
"You keep peeking at the data — this inflates Type I error."▶
This concern applies to frequentist sequential testing, not Bayesian analysis. Updating your posterior as data arrives is correct Bayesian procedure — there is no Type I error inflation from sequential updating (Wald 1947; Rouder 2014).
"The journal requires APA format with p-values."▶
APA 7th edition (2020) explicitly endorses Bayesian statistics: "Interval estimates for effect sizes and Bayesian analyses are encouraged where appropriate." Report both for compliance; frame Bayesian results as the primary interpretive framework.
Writing Bayesian results for a mixed audience▶
Lead with the musical claim first. Translate credible intervals plainly: "We can be 95% confident the true preference rate falls between 63–88%." This is what readers think a confidence interval says — and with credible intervals, it's actually true. Visualize the posterior density.
Summary · Key Takeaways

What Every Music Researcher Should Remember

Bayes inverts probability

Frequentist: P(data | H). Bayesian: P(H | data). The latter is what music researchers actually want.

Prior = prior knowledge

Your literature review is your prior. Every analysis embeds assumptions — Bayes just puts them on the table.

Posteriors accumulate

Today's posterior is tomorrow's prior. Bayesian science is cumulative by design.

Base rates matter

A surprising result in a low-base-rate situation is less credible than it looks. Always consider P(H) before updating.

Report precisely

Never read p-values as hypothesis probabilities. Credible intervals are actual probability statements. Use effect sizes always.

Small samples are honest

Small N produces wide credible intervals — that's honesty, not failure. Incorporate prior knowledge and report uncertainty accurately.

Northwestern Bienen School of Music · Empirical & Critical Methods · shanahdt.github.io/mus-345-445
